coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission ratiometric fluorometric platform using Cu@NCDs/eosin system for sensitive detection of metformin: Pharmacokinetic studies in normal and disease states
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jardan, Yousef A. Bin
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Mostafa, Aya M.
Barker, James
Ali, Almontaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: A novel ratiometric fluorometric method has been developed for the determination of metformin (MET) using a dual-emission system composed of copper ion-modified nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Cu2+/NCDs) and eosin. The sensing mechanism exploits MET’s inherent properties, specifically its strong positive charge and high complexation affinity due to amino groups, which enable simultaneous quenching of eosin fluorescence and restoration of NCDs fluorescence through Cu2+ion complexation. The method demonstrates excellent selectivity owing to its ratiometric sensing mechanism, along with notable linearity and sensitivity in MET detection. Comprehensive characterization of the as-prepared carbon dots was conducted using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques, accompanied by detailed mechanistic studies to elucidate the underlying detection principles. The practical applicability of the method was validated through successful determination of MET in rat plasma samples. Furthermore, the method enabled effective monitoring of MET levels in both healthy rats and those with experimentally induced renal dysfunction, providing valuable insights into the drug’s pharmacokinetic parameters under different physiological conditions. These findings establish this ratiometric fluorometric approach as a promising analytical tool for MET determination in complex biological matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metformin
Ratiometric
Cu2+/NCDs
Eosin
Pharmacokinetics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sample collection, preparation and extraction steps
Structural and morphological studies of NCDs
Optimization of experimental conditions for detection of MTF
Sensing of MET in spiked rat plasma samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through the project number (RSP2025R457).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset: size distribution histogram and HRTEM image), (B) XRD pattern, (C) FTIR spectrum, and (D) XPS spectrum of NCDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence and excitation spectra of NCDs, (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs after exposing to different excitation wavelengths ranging from 260 to 320 nm.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Cu2+/NCDs /EY in the MET concentration range 0 – 10.0 μM. (B) The linear relationship between the (F403/F536) and the MET concentration (0 – 10.0 μM).
(A) Fluorescence spectral overlay, (B) Zeta potential and (C) Absorbance of NCDs, NCDs + Cu2+, NCDs + Cu2++ MET. (D) Reversible cycles after addition of Cu2+ (0.4 mM) and MET (10 μM) over 10 cycles.
Pharmacokinetic profile of MET in healthy rats and rats with renal dysfunction.
Comparison of the reported methods for MET detection with proposed method.
Pharmacokinetic parameters of MET in healthy rats and rats with renal dysfunction (n = 9).

head.ce:title: Dual-emission ratiometric fluorometric platform using Cu@NCDs/eosin system for sensitive detection of metformin: Pharmacokinetic studies in normal and disease states

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451 Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut 71526 Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut 71526, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ratiometric fluorometric method has been developed for the determination of metformin (MET) using a dual-emission system composed of copper ion-modified nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Cu2+/NCDs) and eosin. The sensing mechanism exploits MET’s inherent properties, specifically its strong positive charge and high complexation affinity due to amino groups, which enable simultaneous quenching of eosin fluorescence and restoration of NCDs fluorescence through Cu2+ion complexation. The method demonstrates excellent selectivity owing to its ratiometric sensing mechanism, along with notable linearity and sensitivity in MET detection. Comprehensive characterization of the as-prepared carbon dots was conducted using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques, accompanied by detailed mechanistic studies to elucidate the underlying detection principles. The practical applicability of the method was validated through successful determination of MET in rat plasma samples. Furthermore, the method enabled effective monitoring of MET levels in both healthy rats and those with experimentally induced renal dysfunction, providing valuable insights into the drug’s pharmacokinetic parameters under different physiological conditions. These findings establish this ratiometric fluorometric approach as a promising analytical tool for MET determination in complex biological matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metformin (MET), a first-line oral antidiabetic drug from the biguanide class, works by reducing hepatic glucose production, enhancing peripheral glucose uptake, and improving insulin sensitivity through activation of AMP-activated protein kinase pathway [1]. Typically prescribed at initial doses of 500–850 mg twice daily, with gradual titration up to a maximum of 2550 mg daily based on glycemic response and tolerability [2]. Common side effects include gastrointestinal disturbances (nausea, diarrhea, abdominal discomfort), while the most severe adverse effect is lactic acidosis, particularly in patients with compromised renal function [3]. In renal dysfunction, MET clearance is significantly reduced as the drug is primarily eliminated unchanged through glomerular filtration and tubular secretion, leading to drug accumulation and increased risk of toxicity [4]. This altered pharmacokinetic profile in renal impairment necessitates careful monitoring of MET levels, as changes in key parameters such as volume of distribution, elimination half-life, and drug clearance can significantly impact both therapeutic efficacy and safety margins [5]. Therefore, accurate determination of MET concentrations becomes crucial for optimizing dosing regimens, preventing drug accumulation, and minimizing the risk of lactic acidosis in patients with compromised renal function, making it an essential tool for individualized therapy management and patient safety monitoring [6].

Recent research has focused on developing precise, reliable, and accurate analytical methods for quantifying MET in biological samples, particularly for pharmacokinetic studies and drug interaction investigations. Traditional analytical approaches include sophisticated techniques such as HPLC with ultraviolet detection [7,8], LC-MS/MS [9], and GC–MS [10], often combined with various extraction procedures. Despite their widespread use, these conventional methods present several limitations, including the requirement for expensive instrumentation, complex sample preparation steps involving hazardous organic solvents, and time-consuming analytical procedures. Alternative approaches have explored simpler methodologies, such as spectrophotometric techniques [11,12], which face challenges with sensitivity limitations, and electrochemical methods including square wave and differential pulse voltammetry with preconcentration strategies [13,14]. However, these methods also present their own set of challenges in achieving optimal analytical performance for MET determination. This created a need for developing more efficient, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly analytical solutions that could maintain high sensitivity and accuracy while reducing analysis time and complexity.

The emergence of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as novel nanomaterials has revolutionized the field of analytical chemistry due to their exceptional properties including tunable photoluminescence, excellent photostability, superior water solubility, and remarkable biocompatibility [15,16]. These quantum-sized carbon nanoparticles (typically < 10 nm) have garnered significant attention as versatile fluorescent probes for detecting various analytes ranging from metal ions and small molecules to biomacromolecules [17,18]. The fascinating optical properties of CDs arise from their unique core–shell structure, where the carbonaceous core is surrounded by surface functional groups that can be readily modified to enhance selectivity toward specific analytes [19]. Their cost-effective synthesis from diverse carbon sources, coupled with their resistance to photobleaching and low toxicity, presents significant advantages over traditional organic fluorophores and semiconductor quantum dots. The detection mechanisms typically involve fluorescence “turn-off” or “turn-on” responses triggered by specific molecular interactions, such as electron or energy transfer processes, coordination with metal ions, or structural changes upon analyte binding [20]. Recent advances have demonstrated the successful application of CDs in developing ratiometric fluorescent sensors, which offer more reliable quantitative analysis by providing built-in correction for matrix interferences.

This work introduces an innovative ratiometric fluorometric sensing strategy that uniquely combines copper@ nitrogen doped carbon dots (Cu@NCDs) and eosin (EY) dye for selective determination of MET. The novelty of this approach lies in its dual-signal response mechanism, where MET's distinct molecular characteristics − its positive charge density and strong metal complexation capability through amino groups − are strategically exploited to generate complementary analytical signals. The sensing mechanism operates through simultaneous processes: MET-induced electrostatic quenching of EY fluorescence and copper-MET complexation leading to fluorescence restoration of CDs, providing built-in self-calibration that enhances analytical reliability. This dual-emission system represents a significant advancement over conventional single-signal detection methods, offering improved sensitivity and accuracy particularly crucial for biological applications. The practical utility of this method was demonstrated through successful quantification of MET in rat plasma samples, with special emphasis on comparing pharmacokinetic parameters between normal conditions and renal dysfunction cases, providing valuable insights into drug monitoring in compromised renal function where precise MET level determination is critical for patient safety.

Metformin (MET), sodium hydroxide, cisplatin, phosphoric acid, eosin, glacial acetic acid, 1,10-phenanthroline, boric acid, and salicylic acid were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Cholesterol, glucose, urea, lysine, glimepiride, glipizide, sitagliptin, atorvastatin, furosemide, simvastatin, and lactic acid were supplied from alpha chemika Co., India. Calcium chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium chloride, sodium chloride, ferric chloride, zinc sulphate, nickel sulphate, copper sulphate, cadmium chloride, sodium phosphate and ferrous sulphate were procured from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt. Double distilled water (18.2 MΩ.cm) was used in whole experiments.
All specifics regarding the instrumentation used can be found in the Supplementary Data file.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared via a straightforward hydrothermal approach. The synthesis involved combining 1,10-phenanthroline (25 mg) and salicylic acid (500 mg) in 25 mL of double distilled water, followed by thorough homogenization to ensure complete mixing. The homogeneous mixture was transferred to a 50-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and subjected to thermal treatment at 200℃, maintaining these conditions for 6 h. Post-synthesis purification involved centrifugation of the reaction mixture at 8000 rpm (10-minutes duration), followed by filtration of the supernatant through a 0.22 µm membrane filter to eliminate residual impurities. The final product was obtained through lyophilization and stored at 4℃ for subsequent use.
For a comprehensive explanation of the procedures and calculations, refer to the supplementary data file.

The analytical measurement of MET was performed in a 10 mL volumetric flask by sequentially adding 1.0 mL of the synthesized NCDs solution (100.0 μg mL−1), copper sulfate heptahydrate (1.0 mL, 4 mM), and EY solution (1.0 mL, 500 μg mL−1). Aliquots (0.5 mL) of varying MET concentrations were introduced to this mixture, and the final volume was brought to 10 mL using Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer maintained at pH 7.0. The resulting solution was allowed to equilibrate for 2.0 min before fluorescence analysis. Dual emission signals were monitored at 403 nm and 536 nm upon excitation at 295 nm. A reference solution, prepared identically but without MET addition, served as the blank measurement for the analytical system.

Clinically healthy nine male and female Sprague–Dawley rats (8–10 weeks old), weighing between 200–250 g, were used for this experiment. The animals were housed individually in polypropylene metabolic cages (dimensions suitable for rats, e.g., 30 cm × 20 cm × 18 cm). Rats were provided a commercial pelleted rodent diet (procured from a certified supplier) and water ad libitum. The room temperature was maintained at 25 ± 2°C, with a 12-hour light–dark cycle and controlled humidity levels (50–60 %). The animals were allowed to acclimatize to the experimental environment for 7 days, during which they were monitored daily for health status and general behavior. The rats were monitored throughout the experimental period for food intake, water consumption, body weight, and general health.

Chronic renal failure (CRF) was induced in Sprague–Dawley rats by administering cisplatin at a dose of 6 mg/kg/day intravenously for 7 consecutive days, adapted based on body surface area scaling from previous chronic induction studies in goats [21,22]. Cisplatin was dissolved in sterile saline and delivered via the tail vein. Following treatment, serum biomarkers such as creatinine, along with urine analysis for proteinuria, were evaluated to confirm renal impairment and ensure the occurrence of CRF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample collection, preparation and extraction steps
ce:section.ce:para: Blood samples (1 mL) were collected from the tail vein of each Sprague–Dawley rat at 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 3, 5, 7, 10, 14, 18, and 24 h after the administration of MET. Samples were collected in heparinized microcentrifuge tubes and immediately centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to separate the plasma. Protein precipitation was achieved by mixing plasma aliquots (100 μL) with ice-cold acetonitrile (400 μL) in polypropylene microcentrifuge tubes, followed by brief vortex (10 s). The mixture was then subjected to centrifugation (14,000 rpm, 10 min), and the resulting supernatants were maintained on ice until analysis.

Pharmacokinetic analysis was performed using pK solver software, applying a one-compartment model. The assessed parameters included the area under the concentration–time curve from zero to 24 h (AUC0→24) and to infinity (AUC0→∞), maximum plasma concentration (Cmax), elimination rate constant (Kel), distribution half-life, volume of distribution, clearance (CL), and mean residence time (MRT). Results were expressed as mean values with their corresponding standard deviations. Statistical comparison between groups was conducted using t-test analysis via GraphPad Prism 9.0, with statistical significance established at p < 0.05 for differences between paired datasets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological studies of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1
A shows TEM images along with the particle size distribution of NCDs. The TEM analysis demonstrates that the NCDs possess a spherical structure with average sizes ranging approximately from 3 to 4 nm. Additionally, the particle size distribution analysis reveals that their diameters vary between 1 and 7 nm, highlighting a variation in size distribution among the NCDs. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the NCDs, presented in the inset of Fig. 1 A, revealed a distinct lattice spacing of 0.352 nm. This spacing corresponds to the (002) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon, indicating the presence of a graphitic-like structure within the NCDs. The XRD analysis of NCDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered at 21.95°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphene-like structures ( Fig. 1 B). The broad nature of this peak indicates a low degree of crystallinity, which is characteristic of the amorphous or partially graphitized structure of NCDs [23]. The FTIR spectrum of NCDs revealed several characteristic functional groups essential to their structure ( Fig. 1 C). The broad peak at 3135 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and/or N-H groups, indicating the presence of surface hydroxyl groups and amino functionalities [24]. The small peak observed at 2800 cm−1 corresponds to C-H stretching vibrations from aliphatic groups. The strong absorption band at 1653 cm−1 is characteristic of C=O stretching vibrations from carbonyl groups and C=N stretching from the phenanthroline moiety, while the peak at 1317 cm−1 can be assigned to C-N stretching vibrations [25]. The presence of C-O stretching vibrations is confirmed by the peak at 1091 cm−1, suggesting the incorporation of oxygen-containing functional groups. Finally, the small peak at 704 cm−1 is indicative of aromatic C-H out-of-plane bending vibrations [26], confirming the presence of aromatic structures in the carbon dots. The surface composition and chemical states of the synthesized NCDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full-scan XPS spectrum exhibited three characteristic peaks at 286.57 eV, 399.38 eV, and 532.4 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively ( Fig. 1 D), confirming the successful incorporation of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen into the NCDs structure. Deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s spectrum revealed four distinct peaks: the peak at 284.20 eV is attributed to sp2 C=C bonds, 285.81 eV corresponds to C-N/C-O bonds, and 288.58 eV represents C=O/C=N bonds (
Fig. S1
A). The N1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.78 eV (pyridinic N), 400.23 eV (pyrrolic N), and 402.56 eV (graphitic N), indicating successful nitrogen doping with various bonding configurations (
Fig. S1
B). The O1s spectrum showed three characteristic peaks at 531.73 eV (C=O), 532.92 eV (C-OH), and 533.71 eV (C-O-C) [27], demonstrating the presence of diverse oxygen-containing functional groups on the NCDs surface (
Fig. S1
C).

Spectroscopic characterization of the synthesized NCDs revealed distinctive optical features essential for their sensing capabilities. The absorption profile displayed characteristic bands at 226 and 265 nm in the UV–visible region ( Fig. 2
A), originating from electronic transitions within the carbon core (π-π* of sp2 hybridized domains) and surface-state transitions (n-π*) involving oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups, respectively [28]. Investigation of photoluminescent properties demonstrated remarkable emission characteristics, with a prominent peak centered at 403 nm under 295 nm excitation. Notably, while varying the excitation wavelength between 260–320 nm resulted in intensity modulation of the emission signal ( Fig. 2 B), the wavelength-dependent behavior guided the selection of 295 nm as the optimal excitation wavelength. The reason for selecting 295 nm as the excitation wavelength, despite its lower intensity, is that it produces a more red-shifted emission. This shift helps to minimize interference, making it a more suitable choice for the study. The superior photoluminescent properties of these NCDs were further confirmed by their impressive quantum yield of 34.75 %, highlighting their potential as effective fluorescent probes for analytical applications.

A comprehensive investigation of the NCDs' stability was conducted under diverse experimental conditions. The fluorescent properties exhibited remarkable tolerance to ionic strength variations, maintaining consistent emission intensity across NaCl concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 2.0 M (
Fig. S2
A). Photostability testing revealed sustained fluorescence intensity during the first 150 min of continuous UV irradiation (365 nm), followed by a gradual decrease in emission intensity over the remaining 100-minute exposure period (
Fig. S2
B). The influence of pH on fluorescence behavior showed optimal performance within pH 5.0–9.0, with diminished responses observed in strongly acidic and basic conditions (
Fig. S2
C). Thermal stability assessment demonstrated reliable fluorescence characteristics between 20–80 °C, although notable intensity changes emerged starting from 60 °C (
Fig. S2
D). These findings collectively establish the robust nature of the synthesized NCDs, particularly their stability under moderate temperatures and varying ionic environments, with optimal performance in near-neutral conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for detection of MTF
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the optimal conditions for the dual-emission system comprising Cu2+/NCDs and EY, the fluorescence response was evaluated as the ratio of emission intensities at 403 nm (restored fluorescence of Cu2+/NCDs) to 536 nm (fluorescence of EY). The optimal response corresponded to the highest ratio, indicating maximum fluorescence restoration of Cu2+/NCDs and effective quenching of EY. The effect of pH on the system's interactions with MET was systematically investigated across a pH range of 3.0 to 10.0 (
Fig. S3
A). At lower pH values (pH < 6.5), the quenching efficiency of EY diminished due to reduced negative charge (–COO−), weakening its electrostatic interaction with MET. Additionally, the ability of MET to form a complex with Cu2+ was compromised due to the protonation of its amine groups, resulting in less effective fluorescence restoration of Cu@NCDs. Conversely, at higher pH values (pH > 7.5), MET began losing its positive charge as the –NH3 + groups deprotonated to –NH2, reducing its interaction with EY. Furthermore, the risk of Cu2+ ion precipitation as hydroxides increased, disrupting the fluorescence restoration of Cu2+/NCDs. The highest fluorescence ratio (403 nm/530 nm) was observed at pH 7.0, where MET remained sufficiently protonated (–NH3 +) to interact electrostatically with EY and retained strong binding affinity with Cu2+. This balance ensured efficient operation of the dual-emission system, with maximum response achieved at pH 7.0. The effect of reaction time was systematically investigated over a range of 1.0 min to 15 min to evaluate its impact on the system's performance (
Fig. S3
B). The results revealed that a reaction time of two minutes was sufficient to achieve the optimal response, with no significant improvement observed with extended durations. The optimization of Cu2+ concentration was critical to achieve efficient quenching of carbon dots while maintaining EY's fluorescence integrity. Systematic investigation across a concentration range of 1.0–6.0 mM revealed that 4.0 mM Cu2+ provided optimal quenching efficiency without interfering with EY's fluorescence properties. As illustrated in Fig. S3
C, the fluorescence response of EY remained unaffected until the concentration of Cu2+ exceeded 400 μM. Consequently, this concentration was chosen, as it achieved maximum quenching of NCDs without compromising the fluorescence of EY. The EY concentration was subsequently investigated, with 50 μg mL−1 identified as the optimal concentration providing sufficient signal intensity while maintaining system linearity and avoiding inner filter effects on NCDs. As shown in Fig. S3
D, the fluorescence response of NCDs remained stable up to an EY concentration of 500μg mL−1. Therefore, this concentration was selected as it provided optimal response without interfering with the fluorescence of NCDs. Although EY exhibits maximum fluorescence excitation at 518 nm, a compromise excitation wavelength was selected to simultaneously excite both fluorophores in the dual-emission system. Despite operating at non-optimal excitation conditions (295 nm) for EY, its inherently high quantum yield and strong fluorescence characteristics ensured adequate signal intensity for reliable measurements.

The sensing mechanism leverages the unique molecular characteristics of MET, particularly its strong positive charge and high complexation affinity especially to Cu2+ ions due to the presence of biguanide moiety with multiple amino groups. The interaction between MET and the dual-emission system manifests through two distinct pathways: first, MET's positive charge facilitates its interaction with the negatively charged EY molecules, resulting in fluorescence quenching of EY. Simultaneously, MET's strong complexation ability enables it to interact with Cu2+ ions associated with the NCDs, leading to the restoration of the intrinsic fluorescence of NCDs that was initially quenched by Cu2+ ions ( Fig. 4
A).

Initially, it was essential to confirm that the fluorescence properties of the NCDs remained unaffected in the presence of EY or MET. As depicted in Fig. S6
A, no significant change in fluorescence intensity was observed, indicating that the NCDs maintained their optical properties in the presence of these compounds. The observed response of NCDs to the addition of Cu2+ ions can be attributed to the presence of functional groups such as carboxylate, hydroxyl, and amino groups. These surface moieties significantly enhance the NCDs' ability to complex with Cu2+ ions, facilitating strong interactions and contributing to the quenching effect which commonly based on photoinduced electron transfer [35]. Zeta potential measurements (Fig. 4 B) revealed a shift from − 4.15 mV for the NCDs to 8.42 mV in the presence of Cu2+, confirming coordination of Cu2+ ions with different groups on the NCDs through electrostatic interactions. When MET was introduced into the Cu2+-CD solution, the Zeta potential decreased to − 3.19 mV, suggesting that Cu2+ ions dissociated from the NCDs to form a stronger complex with MET. To verify that MET interacts with copper ions bound to NCDs more strongly than the NCDs themselves, UV–Vis spectroscopy was employed. The spectrum of NCDs alone exhibited an increase in absorption intensity at 265 nm upon the addition of copper ions, along with the emergence of a well-defined shoulder peak at 295 nm, indicative of Cu2+ coordination on the NCDs' surface (Fig. 4 C). Following the introduction of MET, a decrease in the intensity of both peaks was observed, confirming the displacement of copper ions from the NCDs' surface due to the stronger binding affinity of MET. Reversible sensing behavior is critical for practical applications and for understanding sensor mechanisms. This study demonstrated that the NCDs system maintains “on–off-on” reversibility across multiple cycles. By sequentially adding Cu2+ (0.4 mM) and MET (10 μM) to the NCDs solution, it was observed that fluorescence recovery and quenching efficiencies were maintained up to 95 % after 10 cycles. Fig. 4 D further illustrates this cycle, showing high reversibility, with initial Cu2+ and MET concentrations of 0.4 mM and 10 μM, respectively.

The stronger affinity of Cu2+ for MET can be explained through fundamental coordination chemistry principles. Cu2+ exhibits particularly strong binding to MET due to its electronic configuration (d9) and coordination preferences [36]. As a borderline Lewis acid according to HSAB theory, Cu2+ forms stable complexes with the nitrogen-rich ligand MET, which contains both imine and amine groups. The bidentate nature of MET allows it to form a stable five-membered chelate ring with Cu2+, while the presence of multiple nitrogen donor atoms provides strong σ-donation to the metal center. Additionally, Cu2+ has a high crystal field stabilization energy in its common square planar or distorted octahedral geometries, making it especially favorable for complexation with MET's nitrogen donor atoms [37]. The Jahn-Teller effect, characteristic of Cu2+ complexes, also contributes to the formation of particularly stable coordination compounds [38].

The quenching of EY native fluorescence was found to occur not only through ion-pair complex formation, but also through dynamic quenching, static quenching, or fluorescence resonance energy transfer. To investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism between EY and MET, the Stern-Volmer equation was utilized. To gain deeper insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the Stern-Volmer equation was applied [39]. This equation is represented as: F0/F = 1 + Ksv[C], where F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of MET, respectively. C corresponds to the concentration of MET, while Ksv represents the quenching constant. Results showed that the Ksv decreased with elevating temperature as shown in Fig. S6
B, indicating that the process of quenching is of a static nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of MET in spiked rat plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed approach evaluates the effectiveness of the developed procedures for determining MET concentrations in plasma samples. At therapeutic levels, MET administered intravenously is reported to have plasma concentrations ranging from 10 to 20 μM [2]. The method was successfully applied to quantify MET concentrations in plasma samples collected from rats, with the determined range falling between 0.2 and 10.0 μM (
Fig. S7
). The regression equations derived from the analysis are as follows:
F 403
/
F 536
=
0.56 C + 0.47
R 2
= 0.9977
The LOD was determined to be 62.97 nM.

The developed spectrofluorometric method was effectively utilized for the quantification of MET in plasma samples obtained from healthy rats as well as rats with experimentally induced renal dysfunction. This application was designed to facilitate pharmacokinetic analysis. The calibration ranges established for the method were appropriate for analyzing plasma samples collected following the co-administration of MET at a dose of 77 mg for 250 g rat. The plasma concentration levels, both maximum and minimum, from the pharmacokinetic study were found to fall well within the validated calibration range of the method for both conditions. The pharmacokinetic profile of MET in healthy and renal-impaired rats is illustrated in Fig. 5 , while the detailed pharmacokinetic parameters computed from the study are presented in Table 2 . The pharmacokinetic analysis of MET in healthy rats and rats with induced renal dysfunction reveals significant alterations in drug disposition due to impaired renal function. The elimination half-life (t1/2k10) increased markedly in rats with renal dysfunction (10.569 h) compared to healthy rats (5.837 h), indicating a slower elimination process. This finding is further supported by the substantial reduction in apparent clearance, which decreased from 3.0318 (mg)/(μM)/h in healthy rats to 1.283 (mg)/(μM)/h in renal-impaired rats, reflecting compromised renal excretion. Additionally, the volume of distribution was lower in renal dysfunction (19.568 (mg)/(μM)) compared to healthy rats (25.531 (mg)/(μM)), suggesting alterations in tissue distribution or fluid balance due to renal impairment. The maximum plasma concentration was elevated in renal dysfunction (21.045 μM vs. 14.866 μM), likely due to decreased drug elimination, while the time to reach maximum concentration (Tmax) was slightly delayed (2.958 h vs. 2.321 h). Notably, the area under the curve (AUC0–24 and AUC0→∞) was significantly higher in renal dysfunction (303.081 μM.h and 389.609 μM.h, respectively) compared to healthy rats (154.178 μM.h and 164.9171 μM.h), indicating prolonged systemic exposure to MET. The mean residence time also nearly doubled in renal dysfunction (16.269 h vs. 9.362 h), further emphasizing the delayed clearance and extended retention of the drug in the body. These results underscore the critical impact of renal function on MET pharmacokinetics, highlighting the need for careful dose adjustments in cases of renal impairment to prevent drug accumulation and potential toxicity.

In conclusion, this study introduces a novel ratiometric fluorometric method for the determination of metformin using a dual-emission system based on Cu@NCDs and eosin. The method offers several key advantages, including high selectivity due to its ratiometric nature, excellent analytical performance in terms of linearity and sensitivity, and strong applicability to complex biological matrices. The thorough characterization of the carbon dots and in-depth investigation of the detection mechanism provide a robust understanding of the sensing principles. The successful application of this method in monitoring metformin levels in both healthy rats and those with renal dysfunction confirms its utility for pharmacokinetic studies. The ability to accurately quantify metformin concentrations in rat plasma samples demonstrates the method's potential for clinical applications, particularly in therapeutic drug monitoring. The pharmacokinetic results from this study highlight the significant differences in metformin’s distribution, clearance, and half-life between healthy and renal-impaired rats, which may inform future clinical strategies for adjusting metformin dosages based on renal function. It is recommended that further investigations explore the potential of this method in human studies, particularly in individuals with varying degrees of renal impairment. The dual-response mechanism, which integrates both fluorescence quenching and enhancement, offers a robust analytical approach, addressing many of the limitations inherent in traditional methods. This research not only advances analytical methodologies for metformin determination but also provides valuable insights into the drug's pharmacokinetic behavior under different physiological conditions, paving the way for improved therapeutic management and clinical diagnostics. Future work should focus on refining this technique for broader clinical use, particularly in monitoring drug levels for personalized treatment plans.

Yousef A. Bin Jardan: Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Visualization, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Aya M. Mostafa: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. James Barker: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Almontaser Bellah H. Ali: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through the project number (RSP2025R457).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112789.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
